entry.content:
Articles in this issue:

  • What would it mean to treat guns the way we treat cars?
  • Cornel West’s pragmatic America
  • How Ukraine’s new weapons reflect a very different battlefield

  • Blue states are responding to Uvalde

General interest news site

Articles in this issue:

  • The Fight for Roe

  • 20 Bold Takes on the Roe Draft Opinion
News and editorial about politics, culture, entertainment, tech, etc. Contains many articles not seen in The Atlantic magazine

entry.title: The Diplomat: 4 Jun, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • The World Has Not Learnt the Lessons of the Tiananmen Square Massacre
  • Taliban Faces Threat From Islamic State, New Resistance
  • China and Australia’s Dueling Pacific Tours Make Final Stops
  • Uzbekistan’s Journalists: ‘Censorship in Our Minds and Hearts’

  • What China Gets Wrong About Southeast Asia
  • Fuel Shortages Threatens Rural Crisis in Laos
  • Is Indonesia’s Grand Experiment with Democracy Coming to an End?
  • Why Japan Is Getting Tough on Russia Now
  • Myanmar’s Total Displaced Population Tops 1 Million, Says UN
  • Uzbekistan’s Soviet Legacy Lives on in Its Treatment of Journalists

  • Japan Air Self-Defense Force Points to Vertigo in Fatal January Crash

  • Ethnic Armed Groups Eye Post-coup Myanmar
  • Australian Prime Minister Albanese Confirms Trip to Indonesia Next Week
  • Pakistani-American Defends Israel Visit Amid Criticism
  • The Problem With India’s Multiple Strategic Dalliances
  • Contrary to Hindutva Claims, India’s Muslim Fertility Rate is Declining Sharply
  • Export Body Casts Doubt on Thailand-Vietnam Rice Cartel Plan
  • The Somad Affair and Singapore’s Battle Against Religious Extremism
  • Michael Kugelman on South Asia’s Overlapping Crises
  • Japan’s Tangled Territorial Dispute With Russia
  • Carrie Lam’s Legacy
  • The New Face of the Islamic State in Southeast Asia
  • The Pamir Powderkeg
  • Sri Lanka PM Invites Protesting Youth to Join Governance
  • Indonesian Governor in Papua Casts Doubt on Plan for New Provinces: Report
  • 1B Meth Pills: East, Southeast Asia Drug Industry Hits Ominous Peak
  • Vietnam’s Growing Strategic Partnerships with European Countries
  • China, Russia Again Veto UN Statement on Myanmar Conflict
  • ‘Delikado’: Saving Palawan from the Chainsaws
  • How India Influences the Quad
The Diplomat is a current-affairs magazine for the Asia-Pacific, with news and analysis on politics, security, business, technology and life across the region.

Global news and current affairs from a European perspective. Best downloaded on Friday mornings (GMT)

entry.title: The Third Pole: 2 Jun, 2022

The Third Pole is a multilingual platform dedicated to promoting information and discussion about the Himalayan watershed and the rivers that originate there.

entry.title: Wired Magazine: 2 Jun, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:

  • The Multifarious Multiplexity of Taika Waititi
  • Bill Gates Is So Over This Pandemic
  • Paradise at the Crypto Arcade: Inside the Web3 Revolution

  • Volodymyr Zelensky and the Art of the War Story
  • Can Social Media Be Redeemed?
  • Welcome to Janelle Monáe’s Dreamworld
  • Do I Really Need to Back Up All My Data?
  • The Intoxicating Pleasure of Conspiratorial Thinking
  • The End of Alcohol
  • Time Got So Much Weirder. The World Needs a New Lexicon
  • This Startup Wants to Get in Your Ears and Watch Your Brain

  • Is Russia’s Largest Tech Company Too Big to Fail?
  • It’s Like GPT-3, but for Code—Fun, Fast, and Full of Flaws

  • ‘I’m the Operator’: The Aftermath of a Self-Driving Tragedy

  • The History—and Disturbing Resurrection—of Black Androids
  • Jacques Vallée Still Doesn’t Know What UFOs Are
  • The Best Way to Learn Online? Be a Lurker
  • Driving While Baked? Inside the High-Tech Quest to Find Out
  • Ada Palmer and the Weird Hand of Progress
  • How Telegram Became the Anti-Facebook
  • Do Good Doorbell Cams Make Good Neighbors?
  • 6 Apps That Take the Grind out of Your Workday
  • They Were ‘Calling to Help.’ Then They Stole Thousands
  • North Korea Hacked Him. So He Took Down Its Internet
  • Here Come the Underdogs of the Robot Olympics
  • Is It OK to Listen to a Butt-Dial Message?
  • This 22-Year-Old Builds Chips in His Parents’ Garage
  • A Grand Unified Theory of Buying Stuff
  • Can Being Reminded of My Death Improve My Life?
  • The Best of CES 2022
  • In Praise of Unglamorous American Invention
  • Covid Will Become Endemic. The World Must Decide What That Means
  • Advice to Startup Founders: Prepare to Fail
  • The Quest to Trap Carbon in Stone—and Beat Climate Change
  • The Morbid War Over Online Obituaries
  • The Wave-Conquering, Metaverse-Crashing Life of Kai Lenny
  • Welcome to Miami, Where All Your Memes Come True!
  • 4 Dead Infants, a Convicted Mother, and a Genetic Mystery
  • The Twitter Wildfire Watcher Who Tracks California’s Blazes
  • The Matrix Is the Best Hacker Movie
  • Welcome to the (Synthetic) Meatspace
  • WIRED Peers Into the Future of Reality
  • The Most Efficient Way to Debug the Simulation
  • What The Matrix Got Wrong About Cities of the Future
Wired is a full-color monthly American magazine, published in both print and online editions, that reports on how emerging technologies affect culture, the economy and politics. Monthly edition, best run at the start of every month.

Popular Science. Monthly magazine. Should be downloaded around the middle of each month.

entry.title: Nature: 2 Jun, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Nature addresses helicopter research and ethics dumping
  • Fix the process that led to Alzheimer’s drug fiasco
  • Molecular ‘Möbius strip’ gives carbon a twist
  • The stark disparity in a virus’s deadly toll on kids
  • Online pet shops are crawling with spiders captured in the wild
  • Extreme storms will grow bigger as the world warms
  • A harmless bird virus shows ominous changes
  • How dazzling bacteria get their rainbow
  • The salamander that can parachute from atop the world’s tallest trees
  • Gene-edited tomatoes, South Asian heatwave and Australian election
  • Monkeypox goes global: why scientists are on alert
  • ‘Mind blowing’ ancient settlements uncovered in the Amazon
  • Why unprecedented bird flu outbreaks sweeping the world are concerning scientists
  • COVID delays are frustrating the world’s plans to save biodiversity
  • Frustration builds over lengthy delay in revamping Mexico’s science law
  • Guardians of the brain: how a special immune system protects our grey matter
  • Chronic pain — why science has scant succour for one in five people
  • Animal power, and how to perpetuate wonder: Books in brief
  • A century of science boycotts
  • Fifty years after UN environment summit, researchers renew call for action
  • Has the ‘great resignation’ hit academia?
  • How engineers and drug developers are working to change childhood cancer’s deadly calculus
  • A down-to-earth approach to climate change
  • Loops simplify a set-up to boost quantum computational advantage
  • Nerve regrowth can be painful
  • The 6G frequency switch that spares scientific services
  • Clues to the identity of the fossil fish Palaeospondylus
  • From the archive: international efforts to curb pollution, and the perils of broadcasting
  • Multilayer 2D insulator shows promise for post-silicon electronics
  • An immune molecule segregates memories in time
  • The road to fully programmable protein catalysis
  • Strongly correlated electron–photon systems
  • Resolving the H i in damped Lyman α systems that power star formation
  • Probing CP symmetry and weak phases with entangled double-strange baryons
  • Magneto-optical trapping and sub-Doppler cooling of a polyatomic molecule
  • Quantum computational advantage with a programmable photonic processor
  • Non-Hermitian chiral phononics through optomechanically induced squeezing
  • Epitaxial single-crystal hexagonal boron nitride multilayers on Ni (111)
  • A tissue-like neurotransmitter sensor for the brain and gut
  • [18F]Difluorocarbene for positron emission tomography
  • Morphology of Palaeospondylus shows affinity to tetrapod ancestors
  • The mosaic oat genome gives insights into a uniquely healthy cereal crop
  • Single-cell eQTL models reveal dynamic T cell state dependence of disease loci
  • People construct simplified mental representations to plan
  • Neuropathic pain caused by miswiring and abnormal end organ targeting
  • CCR5 closes the temporal window for memory linking
  • Gene regulation by gonadal hormone receptors underlies brain sex differences
  • Distinct gene clusters drive formation of ferrosome organelles in bacteria
  • Developmental dynamics of two bipotent thymic epithelial progenitor types
  • Fundamental immune–oncogenicity trade-offs define driver mutation fitness
  • Mitochondrial uncouplers induce proton leak by activating AAC and UCP1
  • Gibbin mesodermal regulation patterns epithelial development
  • MCM complexes are barriers that restrict cohesin-mediated loop extrusion
  • Fast and efficient DNA replication with purified human proteins
Nature is a weekly international multidisciplinary scientific journal publishing peer-reviewed research in all fields of science and technology on the basis of its originality, importance, interdisciplinary interest, timeliness, accessibility, elegance and surprising conclusions. Nauture also provides rapid, authoritative, insightful and arresting news and interpretation of topical and coming trends affecting science, scientists and the wider public.
